input.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Wire max/batch pack byte flags into sync and replicate

Replicate against a fresh target for a repo the size of linux currently
fails with "decode report-status: invalid pkt-len found: short pkt-line 4"
because the receive-pack POST body hits a server limit (or times out)
and the target closes the connection before writing a report-status.
The fix is batching, which the bootstrap strategy already supports and
which replicate's bootstrap-fallback already plumbs to the strategy via
Config.BatchMaxPackBytes -- but the flags were only registered on the
bootstrap subcommand. Replicate and sync silently ignored them.

Changes:

- cmd/git-sync/main.go runSyncLike: register --max-pack-bytes and
  --batch-max-pack-bytes. Update the usage string for sync, replicate,
  and plan accordingly.
- pkg/gitsync/unstable/client.go buildSyncConfig: forward MaxPackBytes
  and BatchMaxPackBytes from AdvancedOptions to syncer.Config. The
  fields already existed on AdvancedOptions; only bootstrap was using
  them.
- internal/syncer/integration_test.go: add TestRun_Integration-
  ReplicateBootstrapBatchesWhenConfigured exercising a replicate call
  with BatchMaxPackBytes set, asserting it reaches the batched
  bootstrap path (batching=true, batch_count>=2) and leaves source
  and target heads matching. This guards the plumbing; regressing
  the forwarding would silently reintroduce the "flag has no effect"
  bug.
- CHANGELOG.md: document the added flags and the unstable
  buildSyncConfig forwarding.

Typical usage for a large initial replicate push:

  git-sync replicate \
    --batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456 \
    --max-pack-bytes 10737418240 \
    ...

splits the push into ~256 MiB batches, each of which the receive-pack
server processes and acknowledges before the next starts.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Committed as `6a219a1`. Now retry the linux push with batching:

```bash
# Restart your entire dev env first if it's still down
cd ../../devenv && mise run dev &

# Back to git-sync, retry with batching. Adjust sizes to taste.
# 256 MiB per batch is usually a safe starting point:
export SRC=https://github.com/torvalds/linux.git
# (new empty target)

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456 \
  --max-pack-bytes 10737418240 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate.log | jq '{pushed, batching, batch_count, planned_batch_count, relay_reason, peak: .measurement.peak_heap_inuse_bytes}'
```

And tail `entire.log` in another terminal so we can catch any server-side error *while* the push runs. If it still hits "short pkt-line 4", the server log will almost certainly show the root cause this time (body size limit, OOM, timeout, raft commit failure).

## Knobs to tune if 256 MiB batches are still too large

```bash
--batch-max-pack-bytes 67108864    # 64 MiB
--batch-max-pack-bytes 16777216    # 16 MiB
```

Smaller batches = more receive-pack POSTs = higher total time but lower peak memory and fewer opportunities to hit a limit.

content.text:
Short answer: with `-v` there's *some* progress (discrete milestone logs), but the server's sideband progress messages — the `Counting objects / Resolving deltas` lines git shows — are currently discarded. That's why a multi-minute push looks frozen.

## What you get today

With `-v`, `bootstrap.Execute` logs at milestone boundaries to stderr:

```
msg="bootstrap fetching refs from source" ref_count=...
msg="bootstrap pushing refs to target" ref_count=...
msg="bootstrap batch push checkpoint" ...
msg="bootstrap batch checkpoint complete" ...
msg="bootstrap batch branch finalized" branch=refs/heads/master
```

So you'll see a line when a batch starts, when it finishes, etc. — but nothing during the actual upload of each pack, and nothing about what the server is doing while it processes the batch.

## What's missing, and why

Two deliberate silencings in the code:

1. **`internal/gitproto/push.go:sendReceivePack`** demuxes sideband but passes `nil` for the progress sink — any "Counting / Compressing / Resolving" lines the server emits on channel 2 go to `/dev/null`.
2. **`buildV1UploadPackBody` / `fetchPackV2`** unconditionally send `no-progress` to the source, telling the source's upload-pack not to bother emitting progress at all.

Neither is conditional on `-v`. So even with verbose, you get no continuous feedback.

## Want me to wire it up?

Minimal change, ~15 lines:

- When `Verbose=true`, stop sending `no-progress` on upload-pack so the source emits progress.
- Route the fetch-side sideband progress channel and the push-side sideband progress channel to `os.Stderr`, prefixed with `source: ` / `target: ` so you can tell them apart.
- Default (non-verbose) stays quiet for scripted/JSON use.

That would give you a live stream during your linux push, something like:

```
source: Counting objects: 10000000, done.
source: Compressing objects: 37% (3712000/10000000)
source: Compressing objects: 100% (10000000/10000000), done.
target: Unpacking objects:  12% (1200000/10000000)
target: Resolving deltas:   58% (5800000/10000000)
```

Worth it? It's genuinely useful for anything over ~30s, and I can keep it behind `-v` so JSON/scripted callers are unaffected.

Stream sideband progress to stderr when -v is set

Long replicate and sync runs against large repos were silent between
bootstrap batch checkpoints because both the source upload-pack and the
target receive-pack sideband progress channels were being discarded.

Changes in internal/gitproto:

- push.go: sendReceivePack now takes a verbose flag and wires the
  sideband demuxer's Progress field to stderr (prefixed "target: ")
  when set. Added a prefixedLineWriter that splits on both '\n' and
  '\r' so in-place git progress updates ("Resolving deltas: 12%\r")
  remain readable when prefixed. progressSink returns nil for
  non-verbose so the demuxer allocates nothing on the hot path.
- fetch.go / refs.go: RefService gains a Verbose field. When true,
  fetchToStoreV1/V2, fetchPackV1/V2, and buildV1UploadPackBody stop
  asking the source to suppress progress (drop the "no-progress"
  upload-request capability and the "no-progress" v2 fetch arg) and
  wire the sideband demuxer's Progress to stderr (prefixed
  "source: ").
- syncer.go: newSession propagates cfg.Verbose to sourceService.Verbose
  right after constructing it. Target-side verbose already flowed
  through gitproto.NewPusher.

New tests:

- TestPrefixedLineWriter covers line splitting on '\n' and '\r',
  mid-line writes that don't emit a trailing prefix, and empty
  writes.
- TestProgressSinkNilWhenNotVerbose locks in the "no allocation
  when quiet" contract.

Existing private fetch tests updated to pass the new verbose
parameter. Commit-graph fetches (FetchCommitGraph) pass verbose=false
because they are short and not user-facing.

With -v, a replicate run now looks like:

  source: Enumerating objects: 120000, done.

  source: Compressing objects:  37% (44400/120000)
  ...

  target: Updating references: 100% (61/61), done.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

That's healthy progress — you're past the slow part. Here's what those logs are actually showing:

## What phase you're in

**21:18:11 → 21:22:02 (4 min): planning**
The `bootstrap batch fetching commit graph` line kicked off the probe phase. Each `source: Enumerating / Counting / Compressing / Total` block is a *separate* upload-pack round-trip to GitHub asking for tree:0 filtered packs along the master history. 13+ of those just to plan checkpoints.

The planning ended with:
```
bootstrap batch planned checkpoint branch=refs/heads/master checkpoint=a13ff0bb selected=1 chain_len=75767
```

Translation: master has a **75,767-commit** chain. Bootstrap picked **one** intermediate checkpoint. So the actual push will split into ~2 receive-pack POSTs for master plus one for tags — not 75k separate pushes, which is good.

**21:22:02 → now: fetching batch 1**
```
source: Enumerating objects: 2011027, done.
source: Counting objects: 100% (204362/204362), done.
source: Compressing objects: 100% (41832/41832), done.
```

That's **~2 million objects** being packaged on GitHub's side for the first batch (up to the `a13ff0bb` checkpoint). You should next see GitHub emit a "Writing objects: X%" progress line as it streams the pack to us — that's the multi-GB download. Then once it arrives, your client relays it to entire's receive-pack, and you'll see the `target:` lines start ("Unpacking objects: X%" / "Resolving deltas: X%").

## What to watch for next

- `source: Writing objects:` progress — pack being streamed from GitHub
- Stall or abrupt silence here = source-side rate-limit or TLS reset
- `target: Unpacking / Resolving deltas: X%` progress — entire-server processing the batch
- Stall here = entire chewing through delta resolution (watch CPU / heap in entire.log)
- A clean `bootstrap batch checkpoint complete ...` log = batch 1 landed, second batch starts
- A final `bootstrap batch branch finalized branch=refs/heads/master` = master is fully on target
- Then tag batches, then done

## Rough time estimate

GitHub has pack-reused a ton ("pack-reused 685000+" per probe fetch) so the actual pack is mostly pre-built. Network is the main bottleneck on the source side. For ~5 GB over a reasonable connection: 10–30 minutes to pull + push + process per batch.

Let it run. If it hangs for more than a few minutes without either side advancing, that's the real failure signal and we'll have a specific phase to debug.

content.text:
Yes, they can be. Checkpoint selection is driven by `--batch-max-pack-bytes` using this heuristic (`internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go:582`):

```
initialSpanCommits = batchMaxPack / 4096   // ~4 KiB per commit
```

At your current **256 MiB**, initial span ≈ **65,536 commits**. Your chain is 75,767 commits, so bootstrap selected 1 checkpoint at ~65k and pushes the remaining ~10k as a second batch. That also explains why you saw **13+ probe fetches** (the "bootstrap batch fetching commit graph" blocks): each one is a binary-search step narrowing down *where* to place that checkpoint.

## What bigger means in practice

| `--batch-max-pack-bytes` | initial span | likely checkpoints for linux | probe fetches | push payloads |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 256 MiB (now) | ~65k commits | 1 | many | ~2 × 2-3 GiB |
| 512 MiB | ~131k commits | **0** | few | 1 × ~5 GiB |
| 1 GiB | ~262k commits | 0 | 1-2 | 1 × ~5 GiB |
| 2 GiB | ~524k commits | 0 | 1 | 1 × ~5 GiB |
| unset | unbatched | 0 | 0 | 1 × ~5 GiB |

512 MiB or above likely collapses to a single master push with no checkpoint subdivision, which should cut ~3 minutes out of the planning phase.

## What to try

For a clean retry against an empty target:

```bash
time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 1073741824 \
  --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-1g.log
```

Bonus: bootstrap has an **auto-shrink** safety net (`autoBatchMaxPackBytes` in `bootstrap.go:132`). If the first push gets rejected for size, it will retry with smaller batches automatically. So starting big is low-risk.

## The real question: can entire's receive-pack handle a single ~5 GiB push?

That's the unknown. With `--batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456` the ongoing run will tell us if even 256 MiB batches push OK. If that works, try 1 GiB next time. If 256 MiB batches *don't* survive, bigger won't help either and we'll need to look at entire's limits (connect-go message size, raft WAL ingestion, etc.).

Easiest data point for right now: **let the current run finish or fail**. If it finishes, you know 256 MiB works end-to-end and you can try bigger on the next empty-target run.
what does pack-reused mean exactly where does this come from?

Those are git-core's own `pack-objects` counters, rendered on sideband channel 2 during upload-pack. They're not go-git — they come from GitHub's git binary. Format:

```
Total N (delta D), reused R (delta Dr), pack-reused P (from C)
```

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Total N** | total objects being placed in the response pack |
| **delta D** | how many of those are stored as delta objects (vs. whole/base objects) |
| **reused R** | objects found in an existing packfile that the server could emit without recompressing; still had to inspect/rewrite their headers |
| **delta Dr** | how many of those `reused` were delta-encoded (almost always equals `reused` — servers prefer not to materialize deltas) |
| **pack-reused P** | objects copied **byte-for-byte** from an existing packfile's data stream, zero inspection, zero recompression |
| **from C** | how many source packfiles the `pack-reused` bytes came from |

## Why it matters for your run

Look at one of your lines:

```
Total 702386 (delta 15429), reused 14062 (delta 14062), pack-reused 685362 (from 1)
```

- 702,386 total objects in the pack being sent
- 14,062 objects (2%) needed the "inspect-and-emit" path
- **685,362 objects (97.6%) were copied raw** from one pre-built pack
- so GitHub essentially hit a precomputed reachability slice and streamed those bytes verbatim

That's the optimization tier:

```
fastest: pack-reused   ← zero-cost byte copy
medium:  reused        ← existing object, write fresh header
slowest: fresh encode  ← enumerate, delta, compress from scratch
```

## Where the code is

In git-core it's `builtin/pack-objects.c`. The summary line is written by `write_pack_file` → the `progress` variable at end-of-pack. Search for `pack-reused` in that file if you ever want to read the producer.

go-git also has its own implementation in `plumbing/transport/upload_pack.go` — grep for `reused`:

content.text:
Right — **go-git doesn't implement pack-reuse at all**. Its upload-pack always enumerates and re-encodes from scratch. Another reason GitHub serves big repos in seconds while a go-git-based source would take much longer for the same closure.

## Takeaway for your replicate run

The high `pack-reused` ratio is why your 13 probe fetches each finished quickly despite moving 500k+ objects per probe — GitHub wasn't really "counting" anything, it was just streaming a prebuilt pack-slice byte-for-byte. The planning time was dominated by network, not server CPU. So on the source side your throughput ceiling is just your pipe to GitHub.

The receiving side is where it can get expensive: **entire's receive-pack has to decompress, verify, resolve, and commit every one of those objects to its raft-backed storage**, so the `target: Unpacking / Resolving deltas` progress you'll see next is doing real work, not a byte copy. That's where batch sizing matters.

content.text:
This is a good investigation question. The answer is **moderate effort, not hard** — the core plumbing already supports different source and target refs. Here's exactly where it works today and where it doesn't.

## What already works

The `DesiredRef` model and all downstream strategy code (plans, relay, push) fully support `SourceRef ≠ TargetRef`. The `--map` flag proves this end-to-end:

```bash
# This already works — pushes source refs/heads/main to target refs/heads/stable
git-sync replicate --map refs/heads/main:refs/heads/stable $SRC $TGT
```

Push commands just send whatever `TargetRef` the plan says. Receive-pack accepts any valid ref under `refs/`.

## What blocks `refs/gh/` prefixing today

**1. No bulk prefix transform — only 1:1 mappings** (`internal/planner/planner.go:53-73`)

Without `--map`, `BuildDesiredRefs` identity-maps all refs (source = target). With `--map`, you get explicit pairs. There's no `--map-prefix refs/:refs/gh/` to transform all matched refs in bulk.

**2. Tags always identity-map** (`planner.go:76-85`)

```go
// Tags bypass --map entirely
if err := addManaged(refName, refName, RefKindTag, hash); err != nil {
```

Even with mappings, tags always go to the same ref name. A prefix feature would need to intercept this.

**3. Validation rejects unknown ref kinds** (`internal/validation/validation.go:82-89`)

```go
if srcKind == "" {
    return ..., fmt.Errorf("unsupported source ref kind: %s", src)
}
```

`refKind()` only recognizes `refs/heads/` and `refs/tags/`. A ref like `refs/gh/heads/main` returns `""` → rejected.

**4. Relay eligibility checks branch/tag patterns** (`internal/planner/relay.go:126-138`)

```go
case RefKindBranch:
    if !plan.SourceRef.IsBranch() || !plan.TargetRef.IsBranch() {
        return false, "replicate-non-branch-mapping"
    }
```

`IsBranch()` is `strings.HasPrefix(ref, "refs/heads/")`. A `refs/gh/heads/main` target fails this.

## What you'd need to change

| Change | Effort | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Add `--target-prefix refs/gh/` flag (CLI + types) | easy | none |
| Apply prefix in `BuildDesiredRefs` before `addManaged` for both branches and tags | easy | low |
| Relax `refKind` / `NormalizeMapping` to accept custom namespaces or introduce `RefKindCustom` | medium | need to audit all callers |
| Relax relay eligibility to use a ref's *logical* kind (branch/tag based on source) rather than checking target ref prefix | medium | must verify receive-pack handles non-standard refs correctly |
| Prune logic: decide what "managed" means in the prefixed namespace | design decision | depends on semantics |

Nothing in the git protocol or receive-pack cares about ref naming — any ref under `refs/` works. So it's purely planner/validation/relay changes.

## Rough implementation sketch

```go
// In BuildDesiredRefs, after determining targetRef:
if cfg.TargetPrefix != "" {
    // refs/heads/main → refs/gh/heads/main
    targetRef = plumbing.ReferenceName(cfg.TargetPrefix + strings.TrimPrefix(string(sourceRef), "refs/"))
}
```

And carry the source-side `RefKind` through to the plan instead of re-deriving it from the target ref name. That single conceptual change (kind comes from source, not target pattern) unlocks arbitrary target namespaces.

## Design questions to settle first

- **Keep original branches on target too, or only prefixed?** If only prefixed, `refs/heads/main` doesn't exist on target — only `refs/gh/heads/main`. If both, you need two plans per ref.
- **Prune semantics**: does `--prune` delete `refs/gh/heads/stale` when source drops `refs/heads/stale`? Probably yes, but only within the prefixed namespace.
- **Tags**: prefix them too (`refs/gh/tags/v1.0`) or keep them at `refs/tags/v1.0`? Or configurable?
- **Multiple sources**: if you later want `refs/gh/` *and* `refs/gl/` (GitHub + GitLab), the prefix becomes per-source. That's a design fork worth thinking about now even if you only implement one source initially.

Overall: **~1 day of focused work**, mostly in planner + validation. No protocol changes, no strategy rewrites. The hardest part is the design decisions, not the code.

Also I never saw a line "target: "

Two separate issues:

**1. Auth token expired mid-run.** The bootstrap planning took hours (all those probe fetches). By the time it started pushing, the `entiredb` OAuth token had expired. The refresh failed (or the refresh token itself expired), so the push got a 401.

Fix: re-login before retrying, and the token will be fresh for the push phase.

```bash
entiredb auth login
```

Then retry. With larger batches (1 GiB), planning is much faster, so the token is less likely to expire before the push.

**2. No `target:` lines.** That's because the push never got far enough to receive sideband progress — it was rejected at the HTTP level (401) before the receive-pack even started processing. If auth had succeeded, you'd see `target: Unpacking objects: ...` etc. during pack processing.

There's also a second reason: entire's receive-pack is go-git-based, and go-git's `transport.ReceivePack` doesn't emit progress messages on sideband channel 2 — the `TODO: support thin-pack` server also doesn't send progress updates during unpacking/resolving. So even with a successful push, you'd see `source:` lines but likely silence on the `target:` side until the report-status comes back. Real git-core servers (GitHub, GitLab, Gitea with git backend) would emit target progress.

**Recommended retry:**

```bash
entiredb auth login

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 1073741824 \
  --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-2.log
```

The 1 GiB batch size should collapse planning from ~4 min / 13 probes to ~1 min / 1-2 probes, keeping the whole run well within the token's lifetime.

hmm, can you help me understand what the bootstrap planing is doing exactly? Like it taking hours feels like a huge waste, like planing only makes sense if the planing isn't longer then the worst guessed plan? Or can we not just guess something?

Your instinct is right — planning is doing way more work than it should for this case. Here's exactly what's happening and why it's wasteful:

## What the planning phase actually does

```
Step 1: fetch commit graph          → 1 fetch (tree:0 filter, commits only)
        Walk first-parent chain     → 75,767 commits for linux/master

Step 2: binary-search for where to place checkpoint boundaries
        For each probe candidate:
            → actual FetchPack from GitHub   ← THIS IS THE EXPENSIVE PART
            → download the FULL pack
            → measure its size
            → decide: fits under 256 MiB? yes → try wider. no → try narrower.
```

**Every single one of those `source: Enumerating objects: 700000` blocks was a real pack download.** The planner downloaded ~500K-700K objects (~200-300 MiB) per probe, **13+ times**, just to measure where the 256 MiB boundary falls. That's ~3-4 GiB of throwaway data downloaded during planning alone.

The 1 probe result that actually fit gets saved (`prefetched`) and reused for the push. The other 12+ are discarded.

## Why it's designed this way

The comment in `initialCheckpointSpan` explains the thinking:

```go
// Typical compressed commit + tree overhead averages ~2-5 KiB per commit
const avgBytesPerCommit = 4096
```

It starts with a commit-count heuristic (batch limit / 4 KiB), then **refines by measurement** because pack sizes are hard to predict (delta compression, GitHub's pack-reuse, blob sizes vary wildly). The binary search with real fetches is the refinement.

## Why it's wasteful for your case

At 256 MiB limit: `initialSpan = 268435456 / 4096 = 65,536 commits`. Chain = 75,767. So it starts with a guess that's close but doesn't cover the whole chain → binary search kicks in → many probes.

At 1 GiB limit: `initialSpan = 1073741824 / 4096 = 262,144 commits`. That's > 75,767 → the planner tries the whole chain as one batch on the first probe. If it fits (it will — linux master is ~4-5 GiB), done in 1-2 probes.

## The real answer to "can we not just guess?"

**Yes.** There are three progressively simpler approaches:

**A. Just try it and retry on failure (already half-implemented).**
`autoBatchMaxPackBytes` at `bootstrap.go:126` already retries with batching when a push gets rejected. So the optimal strategy for unknown targets is: push the whole thing. If it fails with a body-size error, split and retry. Zero planning probes. The retry path exists but is only triggered on *push rejection*, not used as the primary strategy.

**B. Estimate from commit count, never probe.**
Replace the binary search with pure arithmetic: `numBatches = ceil(chainLen * avgBytesPerCommit / batchLimit)`. Place checkpoints evenly. If a batch turns out too big, the target rejects it and you retry that batch at half size. No probe fetches ever.

**C. Just use large batches.**
Your immediate fix: `--batch-max-pack-bytes 2147483648` (2 GiB) or even omit it entirely and rely on auto-retry. Planning collapses to 1-2 fetches.

## What I'd recommend

For the near term: use a large batch limit (1-2 GiB) and let auto-retry handle target rejections. That's zero code changes and turns your 4-minute planning phase into ~30 seconds.

Longer term: option B (estimate-then-retry) is the right architecture. The probe-and-measure approach was built for precision but the precision isn't worth 13 round-trips to GitHub. A commit-count estimate with a 2x safety margin and a retry-on-rejection fallback would handle linux-sized repos in a fraction of the time. Want me to sketch that out?

String: // --- Checkpoint planning ---

func planBatches(ctx context.Context, p Params, desired []planner.DesiredRef) ([]plannedBatch, error) {
	out := make([]plannedBatch, 0, len(desired))
	for _, ref := range desired {
		checkpoints, prefetched, err := planCheckpointsWithCache(ctx, p, ref)
		if err != nil {
			return nil, err
		}
		out = append(out, plannedBatch{
			BootstrapBatch: planner.BootstrapBatch{
				Plan: planner.BranchPlan{
					Branch: ref.Label, SourceRef: ref.SourceRef,
					TargetRef: ref.TargetRef, SourceHash: ref.SourceHash,
					Kind: ref.Kind, Action: planner.ActionCreate,
				},
				TempRef:     planner.BootstrapTempRef(ref.TargetRef),
				ResumeHash:  p.TargetRefs[planner.BootstrapTempRef(ref.TargetRef)],
				Checkpoints: checkpoints,
			},
			prefetchedPacks: prefetched,
		})
	}
	return out, nil
}

// PlanCheckpoints plans the checkpoint hashes for a single branch during batched bootstrap.
func PlanCheckpoints(ctx context.Context, p Params, ref planner.DesiredRef) ([]plumbing.Hash, error) {
	checkpoints, _, err := planCheckpointsWithCache(ctx, p, ref)
	return checkpoints, err
}

func planCheckpointsWithCache(ctx context.Context, p Params, ref planner.DesiredRef) ([]plumbing.Hash, map[plumbing.Hash][]byte, error) {
	cp, err := newCheckpointPlanner(ctx, p, ref)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, nil, err
	}
	return cp.plan()
}

type probeResult struct {
	tooLarge bool
	data     []byte
}

type checkpointPlanner struct {
	ctx        context.Context
	params     Params
	ref        planner.DesiredRef
	chain      []plumbing.Hash
	probeCache map[string]probeResult
	prefetched map[plumbing.Hash][]byte
}

func newCheckpointPlanner(ctx context.Context, p Params, ref planner.DesiredRef) (*checkpointPlanner, error) {
	p.log("bootstrap batch fetching commit graph", "branch", ref.TargetRef.String())
	graphStore := memory.NewStorage()
	gpRef := gitproto.DesiredRef{SourceRef: ref.SourceRef, TargetRef: ref.TargetRef, SourceHash: ref.SourceHash}
	if err := p.SourceService.FetchCommitGraph(ctx, graphStore, p.SourceConn, gpRef); err != nil {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("fetch bootstrap planning graph for %s: %w", ref.TargetRef, err)
	}
	chain, err := planner.FirstParentChain(graphStore, ref.SourceHash)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("walk first-parent chain for %s: %w", ref.TargetRef, err)
	}
	if len(chain) == 0 {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("empty first-parent chain for %s", ref.TargetRef)
	}
	return &checkpointPlanner{
		ctx:        ctx,
		params:     p,
		ref:        ref,
		chain:      chain,
		probeCache: make(map[string]probeResult),
		prefetched: make(map[plumbing.Hash][]byte),
	}, nil
}

func (p *checkpointPlanner) plan() ([]plumbing.Hash, map[plumbing.Hash][]byte, error) {
	checkpoints := make([]plumbing.Hash, 0, len(p.chain))
	prevIdx := -1
	prevHash := plumbing.ZeroHash
	prevSpan := initialCheckpointSpan(p.params.BatchMaxPack, len(p.chain))
	prevMeasuredBytes := 0
	for prevIdx < len(p.chain)-1 {
		bestIdx, err := p.selectNextCheckpoint(prevIdx, prevHash, prevSpan, prevMeasuredBytes)
		if err != nil {
			return nil, nil, err
		}
		if bestIdx <= prevIdx {
			return nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("could not find bootstrap checkpoint for %s under batch-max-pack-bytes=%d", p.ref.TargetRef, p.params.BatchMaxPack)
		}
		if result, ok := p.cachedProbe(prevHash, bestIdx); ok && !result.tooLarge && len(result.data) > 0 {
			p.prefetched[p.chain[bestIdx]] = result.data
			prevSpan = adaptiveNextProbeSpan(p.params.BatchMaxPack, len(result.data), bestIdx-prevIdx, len(p.chain)-1-bestIdx)
			prevMeasuredBytes = len(result.data)
		} else {
			prevSpan = bestIdx - prevIdx
			prevMeasuredBytes = 0
		}
		prevIdx = bestIdx
		prevHash = p.chain[bestIdx]
		checkpoints = append(checkpoints, prevHash)
		p.params.log("bootstrap batch planned checkpoint",
			"branch", p.ref.TargetRef.String(),
			"checkpoint", planner.ShortHash(prevHash),
			"selected", len(checkpoints),
			"chain_len", len(p.chain))
	}
	return checkpoints, p.prefetched, nil
}

func (p *checkpointPlanner) selectNextCheckpoint(prevIdx int, prevHash plumbing.Hash, prevSpan int, prevMeasuredBytes int) (int, error) {
	bestIdx := -1
	remaining := len(p.chain) - 1 - prevIdx
	if shouldSelectTipWithoutProbe(p.params.BatchMaxPack, prevMeasuredBytes, prevSpan, remaining) {
		bestIdx = len(p.chain) - 1
	} else if shouldProbeTipFirst(p.params.BatchMaxPack, prevMeasuredBytes, prevSpan, remaining) {
		tipIdx := len(p.chain) - 1
		tooLarge, err := p.probe(prevHash, tipIdx)
		if err != nil {
			return -1, err
		}
		if !tooLarge {
			bestIdx = tipIdx
		}
	}
	if bestIdx != -1 {
		return bestIdx, nil
	}
	return p.searchCheckpointUnderLimit(prevIdx, prevHash, prevSpan)
}

func (p *checkpointPlanner) probe(prevHash plumbing.Hash, idx int) (bool, error) {
	if result, ok := p.cachedProbe(prevHash, idx); ok {
		return result.tooLarge, nil
	}
	data, tooLarge, err := fetchPackForProbe(p.ctx, p.params, p.ref, p.chain[idx], prevHash, p.params.BatchMaxPack)
	if err != nil {
		return false, fmt.Errorf("measure bootstrap batch for %s at %s: %w", p.ref.TargetRef, planner.ShortHash(p.chain[idx]), err)
	}
	p.probeCache[probeKey(prevHash, idx)] = probeResult{tooLarge: tooLarge, data: data}
	return tooLarge, nil
}

func (p *checkpointPlanner) cachedProbe(prevHash plumbing.Hash, idx int) (probeResult, bool) {
	result, ok := p.probeCache[probeKey(prevHash, idx)]
	return result, ok
}

func (p *checkpointPlanner) probeBounds(prevHash plumbing.Hash, lo, hi int) (largestFit int, smallestTooLarge int) {
	largestFit = lo - 1
	smallestTooLarge = hi + 1
	for idx := lo; idx <= hi; idx++ {
		result, ok := p.cachedProbe(prevHash, idx)
		if !ok {
			continue
		}
		if result.tooLarge {
			if idx < smallestTooLarge {
				smallestTooLarge = idx
			}
			continue
		}
		if idx > largestFit {
			largestFit = idx
		}
	}
	return largestFit, smallestTooLarge
}

func (p *checkpointPlanner) searchCheckpointUnderLimit(prevIdx int, prevHash plumbing.Hash, prevSpan int) (int, error) {
	lo := prevIdx + 1
	hi := len(p.chain) - 1
	if lo > hi {
		return -1, nil
	}

	largestFit, smallestTooLarge := p.probeBounds(prevHash, lo, hi)
	if smallestTooLarge > hi {
		if largestFit >= lo {
			return largestFit, nil
		}

		idx := nextCheckpointProbeCandidate(lo, hi, prevSpan, largestFit, smallestTooLarge)
		tooLarge, err := p.probe(prevHash, idx)
		if err != nil {
			return -1, err
		}
		if !tooLarge {
			return idx, nil
		}
		smallestTooLarge = idx
	}

	for smallestTooLarge-largestFit > 1 {
		idx := nextCheckpointProbeCandidate(lo, hi, prevSpan, largestFit, smallestTooLarge)
		if idx <= largestFit {
			idx = largestFit + 1
		}
		if idx >= smallestTooLarge {
			idx = smallestTooLarge - 1
		}
		if idx < lo || idx > hi || idx <= largestFit || idx >= smallestTooLarge {
			break
		}

		tooLarge, err := p.probe(prevHash, idx)
		if err != nil {
			return -1, err
		}
		if tooLarge {
			smallestTooLarge = idx
			continue
		}
		largestFit = idx
	}

	if largestFit >= lo {
		return largestFit, nil
	}

	tooLarge, err := p.probe(prevHash, lo)
	if err != nil {
		return -1, err
	}
	if tooLarge {
		return -1, nil
	}
	return lo, nil
}

func probeKey(prevHash plumbing.Hash, idx int) string {
	return prevHash.String() + ":" + strconv.Itoa(idx)
}

func initialCheckpointSpan(batchMaxPack int64, chainLen int) int {
	// Issue #14: Use a commit-count heuristic for the initial span estimate
	// to reduce expensive fetch-and-discard probes. Typical compressed commit
	// + tree overhead averages ~2-5 KiB per commit in a mature repo.
	const avgBytesPerCommit = 4096
	if batchMaxPack <= 0 {
		return 0
	}
	initialSpan := int(batchMaxPack / avgBytesPerCommit)
	if initialSpan > chainLen {
		initialSpan = chainLen
	}
	if initialSpan < 1 {
		initialSpan = 1
	}
	return initialSpan
}

func adaptiveNextProbeSpan(limit int64, measuredBytes int, selectedSpan int, remaining int) int {
	if selectedSpan < 1 {
		selectedSpan = 1
	}
	if remaining < 1 {
		return selectedSpan
	}
	if limit <= 0 || measuredBytes <= 0 {
		if selectedSpan > remaining {
			return remaining
		}
		return selectedSpan
	}
	estimated := int(float64(selectedSpan) * float64(limit) / float64(measuredBytes))
	if estimated < 1 {
		estimated = 1
	}
	if estimated > remaining {
		estimated = remaining
	}
	return estimated
}

func shouldProbeTipFirst(batchMaxPack int64, prevMeasuredBytes int, prevSpan int, remaining int) bool {
	if batchMaxPack <= 0 || remaining <= 0 {
		return false
	}
	if prevMeasuredBytes > 0 && prevSpan > 0 {
		estimated := int64(float64(prevMeasuredBytes) * float64(remaining) / float64(prevSpan))
		return estimated <= batchMaxPack
	}
	return false
}

func shouldSelectTipWithoutProbe(batchMaxPack int64, prevMeasuredBytes int, prevSpan int, remaining int) bool {
	if batchMaxPack <= 0 || remaining <= 0 {
		return false
	}
	if prevMeasuredBytes > 0 && prevSpan > 0 {
		estimated := int64(float64(prevMeasuredBytes) * float64(remaining) / float64(prevSpan))
		return estimated <= batchMaxPack/2
	}
	return false
}

func nextCheckpointProbeCandidate(lo, hi, prevSpan, largestFit, smallestTooLarge int) int {
	if largestFit >= lo && smallestTooLarge <= hi {
		return (largestFit + smallestTooLarge) / 2
	}
	if largestFit >= lo {
		return largestFit + prevSpan
	}
	projected := lo + prevSpan - 1
	if projected > hi {
		return hi
	}
	return projected
}

func fetchPackForProbe(ctx context.Context, p Params, ref planner.DesiredRef, want, have plumbing.Hash, limit int64) ([]byte, bool, error) {
	desired := singleGP(ref.SourceRef, ref.TargetRef, want)
	haves := planner.SingleHaveMap(have)
	packReader, err := p.SourceService.FetchPack(ctx, p.SourceConn, desired, haves)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, false, err
	}
	defer packReader.Close()
	data, err := io.ReadAll(gitproto.LimitPackReader(packReader, limit))
	if err == nil {
		return data, false, nil
	}
	if strings.Contains(err.Error(), "source pack exceeded max-pack-bytes limit") {
		return nil, true, nil
	}
	return nil, false, err

## Context
The current planner (`planCheckpointsWithCache` → `checkpointPlanner` → binary-search probes via `fetchPackForProbe`) does full FetchPack round-trips per probe candidate to measure actual pack size. For linux/master (75k commits) this takes 13+ fetch-and-discard cycles, downloading gigabytes of throwaway data before any real push starts. The fix is to estimate pack size from commit count and place checkpoints evenly — no probes.

## What to change in bootstrap.go

1. Replace `planBatches` to call a new `planCheckpointsFromChain` instead of `planCheckpointsWithCache`. The `plannedBatch` struct no longer needs `prefetchedPacks` (set it to nil).

2. Replace `PlanCheckpoints` (public) to call `planCheckpointsFromChain`.

3. Add `planCheckpointsFromChain(ctx, p, ref)` which:
   - Fetches the commit graph (same as old `newCheckpointPlanner` did — `FetchCommitGraph` + `FirstParentChain`)
   - Calls `estimateBatchCount(int64(len(chain)), p.BatchMaxPack)` to get number of batches
   - Calls `evenCheckpoints(chain, numBatches)` to place them evenly
   - Logs: `"bootstrap batch planned checkpoints"` with `branch`, `chain_len`, `estimated_batches`
   - Returns `([]plumbing.Hash, error)` — no prefetched map

4. Add helper functions:
   ```go
   const estimatedBytesPerCommit = 8192
   
   func estimateBatchCount(chainLen int64, batchMaxPack int64) int {
       if batchMaxPack <= 0 || chainLen <= 0 { return 1 }
       estimated := chainLen * estimatedBytesPerCommit
       n := int((estimated + batchMaxPack - 1) / batchMaxPack)
       if n < 1 { n = 1 }
       return n
   }
   
   func evenCheckpoints(chain []plumbing.Hash, numBatches int) []plumbing.Hash {
       if numBatches <= 1 || len(chain) <= 1 {
           return []plumbing.Hash{chain[len(chain)-1]}
       }
       checkpoints := make([]plumbing.Hash, 0, numBatches)
       batchSize := len(chain) / numBatches
       for i := 0; i < numBatches-1; i++ {
           idx := (i+1)*batchSize - 1
           if idx >= len(chain)-1 { break }
           checkpoints = append(checkpoints, chain[idx])
       }
       checkpoints = append(checkpoints, chain[len(chain)-1])
       return checkpoints
   }
   ```

## What changed

**Before**: 13+ full `FetchPack` round-trips to GitHub (each downloading ~300 MiB of pack data, discarded after measuring) just to decide where to place checkpoint boundaries. ~4 minutes of planning for linux/master.

**After**: 1 commit-graph fetch (~20s) → count commits → `ceil(75767 × 8KiB / batchLimit)` → evenly-spaced checkpoints. Zero probe fetches.

content.text: The fix is straightforward: use `MaxPackBytes` (user's hard abort, default 0 = unlimited) for the per-fetch limit, not `BatchMaxPack`. `BatchMaxPack` stays as the planning divisor only.

input.command:
git add internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Use MaxPackBytes not BatchMaxPack as per-fetch abort threshold

BatchMaxPack controls checkpoint placement (how many batches to plan).
MaxPackBytes is the user's hard abort threshold for any single source
fetch. The previous code used min(BatchMaxPack, MaxPackBytes) as the
per-fetch limit, which meant a batch whose actual pack exceeded the
planning estimate was aborted at the source-fetch stage before the
target even saw it.

With estimate-based planning, the estimate can undercount (8 KiB/commit
vs linux's real ~66 KiB/commit), so a batch legitimately exceeds the
planning heuristic. The fetch should still complete — if the resulting
pack is too large for the target's receive-pack, the push fails and
bootstrap resume handles retry. Aborting early at the source side just
wastes the transfer.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Now retry — planning will be fast, and the fetch won't abort at 1 GiB:

```bash
entiredb auth login

  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 1073741824 \
  --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-4.log
```

This time the ~5 GiB fetch from GitHub will complete (capped at 20 GiB, not 1 GiB), and the full pack gets relayed to entire in one push. If entire's receive-pack can handle it, done. If it can't (body-size limit, OOM), the error will come from the *target* side this time, which is actionable — lower `--batch-max-pack-bytes` to force more checkpoints.
I think the target has a 2Gb limit

Then split into more batches. At 8 KiB/commit estimate for 75k commits, you need to lower the batch limit so the estimate produces enough batches to keep real pack size under 2 GiB each.

Real pack is ~5 GiB for 75k commits → ~66 KiB/commit actual. To get batches under 2 GiB:

```
75k commits × 66 KiB ≈ 5 GiB total
5 GiB / 2 GiB = 3 batches needed
```

But our estimate uses 8 KiB/commit, so we need to set the batch limit low enough that `ceil(75k × 8KiB / limit) ≥ 3`:

```
75k × 8KiB = 600 MiB estimated
600 MiB / 3 = 200 MiB per batch → yields 3 batches
```

Each batch covers ~25k commits → actual ~1.7 GiB → under the 2 GiB target limit.

```bash
  --measure-memory --stats --json \

```

That's 200 MiB batch limit → 3 estimated batches → ~1.7 GiB actual per batch → under 2 GiB.

Progress! Batch 1 (307 MiB) and batch 2 (1.4 GiB) landed. Batch 3 hit the 2 GiB limit — the last third of the chain has more objects than the first two thirds combined (6.5M vs 4.9M objects). The commit graph isn't uniform.

Good news: **resume will pick up from batch 2**. The temp ref `refs/gitsync/bootstrap/heads/master` is sitting at `3eab887a`. Just rerun with a smaller batch limit so the remaining ~50k commits get split into more pieces:

```bash
time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 104857600 \
  --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-6.log
```

100 MiB batch limit → the remaining chain will split into ~6 batches of ~1 GiB actual each. The first 2 batches (already pushed) get skipped via resume.

The underlying issue is that object density varies wildly along the commit chain — early history is sparse, recent history is dense. Evenly spacing by commit count doesn't produce even pack sizes. That's a known limitation of the estimate approach. A future improvement could weight checkpoints by commit density (merge commits, file-count-per-commit from the graph), but for now, smaller batch limits are the safe path.
but I thought we would just retry in that case with a smaller batch automatically?

234					TargetRef: batch.TempRef, SourceHash: checkpoint, TargetHash: current,
235					Kind: batch.Plan.Kind, Action: planner.ActionForTargetHash(current),
236					Reason: fmt.Sprintf("%s -> %s via %s", planner.ShortHash(current), planner.ShortHash(checkpoint), batch.TempRef),
237				}}

241						TargetRef: batch.Plan.TargetRef, SourceHash: checkpoint,
242						TargetHash: plumbing.ZeroHash, Kind: batch.Plan.Kind,
243						Action: planner.ActionCreate,

245					})

257				}

261					"batch", idx+1,
262					"batch_total", len(batch.Checkpoints))
263				current = checkpoint
264				result.BatchCount++
265			}
266	
267			if current.IsZero() {
268				return result, fmt.Errorf("bootstrap batching for %s completed with no checkpoint state", batch.Plan.TargetRef)
269			}
270			if batch.ResumeHash == batch.Plan.SourceHash && p.TargetRefs[batch.Plan.TargetRef].IsZero() {

272				if err := p.TargetPusher.PushCommands(ctx, cmds); err != nil {

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Auto-subdivide batches on target body-size rejection

When a batched bootstrap push is rejected by the target for exceeding
its body-size limit, instead of failing immediately, subdivide the
remaining checkpoints by inserting midpoints from the stored commit
chain and retry. Each failing batch gets split in half, halving the
commit range (and roughly halving the pack size) per retry. This
converges to batches small enough for the target without requiring the
user to manually lower --batch-max-pack-bytes and re-run.

The commit chain is already in memory from the planning phase (~1.5 MiB
for linux's 75k commits). subdivideCheckpoints walks the remaining
checkpoint list, computes chain-index gaps, and inserts a midpoint for
each gap > 1 commit. Adjacent commits (gap=1) cannot be split further;
if even a single-commit batch is too large, the push fails as before.

Example for the linux kernel at 200 MiB batch estimate:
  - Estimate plans 3 batches (25k commits each)
  - Batch 3 contains 6.5M objects (~2.3 GiB), target limit is 2 GiB
  - Push rejected → subdivide inserts midpoint → 4 checkpoints
  - Retry batch 3a (~12.5k commits, ~1.2 GiB) → succeeds
  - Continue batch 3b (~12.5k commits, ~1.1 GiB) → succeeds
  - No user intervention needed

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Now retry your linux push. Since batches 1-2 already landed via the temp ref, resume will skip them. Batch 3 will fail at the 2 GiB limit, auto-subdivide, and retry:

```bash
entiredb auth login

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 209715200 \
  --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-6.log
```

You should see something like:

```
bootstrap batch push checkpoint branch=refs/heads/master batch=1 batch_total=3 ...
bootstrap batch checkpoint complete ...   ← batch 1 skipped (resume)
bootstrap batch push checkpoint batch=2 ...
bootstrap batch checkpoint complete ...   ← batch 2 skipped (resume)
bootstrap batch push checkpoint batch=3 ...
source: Enumerating objects: 6523907 ...
bootstrap batch subdividing after target size rejection ...
  old_remaining=1 new_remaining=2
bootstrap batch push checkpoint batch=3 batch_total=4 ...  ← retry first half
source: Enumerating objects: ~3M ...
target: Storing objects: ... done.
bootstrap batch checkpoint complete ...
bootstrap batch push checkpoint batch=4 batch_total=4 ...  ← second half
...
bootstrap batch branch finalized branch=refs/heads/master
```
stupid question: could we figure out while transfering from source to target that the packfile gets to big and then meanigful cut it?

Not a stupid question at all — it's the ideal solution. Instead of guessing batch sizes upfront, you'd stream from source and cut when approaching the target's limit. No estimation, no retry, one pass.

**The hard part**: a git packfile isn't arbitrarily splittable. The pack is a stream of delta-compressed objects where later objects may reference earlier ones as bases. You can't cut at an arbitrary byte offset and have two valid packs — the second half would reference bases that only exist in the first half.

**But**: you CAN cut at a *commit boundary* in the checkpoint chain. The key insight is that the commit chain gives you safe split points. If you're streaming objects for commits A→Z and you've passed the commit-B boundary, you can stop the stream, push what you have as "A→B", then start a new fetch for "B→Z".

The challenge is knowing which objects belong to which commit boundary while the pack is streaming. The source packs objects in an order optimized for delta compression, not in commit order. So "objects for commit B" are interleaved with objects for commits C-Z in the pack.

**What WOULD work**: a counting proxy that tracks bytes streamed and, when approaching the limit, signals the push to complete with a reduced ref update (only advance to the last known-safe checkpoint), then starts a new fetch for the remainder. This is essentially "streaming batching" — you don't know the batch boundaries until you're mid-stream.

Sketch:

```
source fetch(want=tip, have=current)
  → tee through byte counter
  → when counter passes threshold:
      - close the pack stream (source sends remaining objects, we discard)
      - push what we have as current→midpoint
      - start new fetch(want=tip, have=midpoint)
```

The problem: we can't "close early and have a valid pack." The pack header declares the object count upfront. Truncating violates that. The target's receive-pack would reject a truncated pack.

**What could actually work in practice**:

Rather than cutting mid-stream, use the `Content-Length` from the source's HTTP response (if present) to decide *before* pushing whether this batch needs splitting. GitHub sends `Content-Length` on pack responses. Check it, and if it's over the target's limit, abort the fetch, subdivide, and re-fetch smaller ranges. This is one extra HEAD-like round-trip per oversized batch — way cheaper than the old probe approach, and no wasted transfer.

That'd be a one-line check right after `FetchPack` returns, before forwarding to `PushPack`. Want me to look into whether the HTTP response exposes content-length through the reader chain?
oh we can do a HEAD check? then yeah we should totally do that

content.text:
No `Content-Length` — GitHub streams via HTTP/2. But there's something better we can read in **12 bytes**: the PACK header.

Every git packfile starts with `PACK<version:4><object-count:4>`. We can peek at those 12 bytes after FetchPack returns, estimate size from the object count, and abort before downloading the full pack.

```
estimated_size = object_count × ~750 bytes/object (compressed average)
```

For linux batch 3: 6.5M objects × 750 ≈ 4.9 GiB → way over 2 GiB limit → subdivide immediately, zero wasted transfer.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Pre-check PACK header object count before pushing oversized batches

When a batch's FetchPack returns, peek at the first 12 bytes of the
pack stream to read the PACK header's object count. Multiply by 750
bytes/object (a conservative compressed-object average) to estimate
total pack size. If the estimate exceeds BatchMaxPack, subdivide
immediately and re-fetch smaller ranges — avoiding a multi-GiB
transfer that the target would reject anyway.

This is a zero-cost optimization: 12 bytes read + one multiplication.
The PACK header is prepended back to the reader via MultiReader so
the push receives a valid packfile if the check passes.

Combined with the post-push subdivide from the previous commit, the
batch loop now has two layers of protection:
1. Pre-push: PACK header estimate catches obviously-oversized batches
   before any significant transfer (this commit).
2. Post-push: target body-limit rejection triggers subdivide for
   batches that passed the estimate but still exceeded the target's
   actual limit (previous commit).

For the linux kernel against a 2 GiB target:
- Estimate plans 3 batches (even by commit count)
- Batch 3 fetches, PACK header shows 6.5M objects × 750 = ~4.9 GiB
- 4.9 GiB > 2 GiB BatchMaxPack → subdivide before pushing
- Re-fetch two halves (~3.2M objects each, ~2.4 GiB)
- If still over, subdivide again — converges in O(log n) splits

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Now retry. Batches 1-2 already landed (resume). Batch 3 will now be caught by the PACK header check and auto-subdivided before wasting any transfer:

```bash
entiredb auth login

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 209715200 \
  --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-7.log
```

Expected flow:
```
batch 1: skipped (resume)
batch 2: skipped (resume)
batch 3: fetch → PACK header: 6.5M objects × 750 = 4.9 GiB > 200 MiB
  → "subdividing before push (pack header estimate)"
  → re-plan: insert midpoint → now 4 checkpoints
batch 3a: fetch → PACK header: ~3M objects → ~2.2 GiB > 200 MiB
  → subdivide again → 6 checkpoints
batch 3a-i: fetch → ~1.5M objects → ~1.1 GiB > 200 MiB
  → subdivide again ...
  (converges in ~4 splits to batches under 2 GiB)
```

The splits happen in seconds (12-byte reads). Only the final correctly-sized fetches actually transfer data.
but just to be sure, we still try to guess meaningful sizes and then act on pack header, right? so it's still a waste on the source (having to build the pack file) but we still try to avoid it

Exactly right. Two layers:

1. **Estimate upfront** (commit-count heuristic): `ceil(75k × 8KiB / batchLimit)` → place checkpoints evenly. This avoids most oversized fetches by starting with a reasonable split. Zero cost — just arithmetic on the chain length we already have.

2. **PACK header precheck** (12-byte peek): catches the cases where the estimate was wrong. Costs the source a full `pack-objects` run (it builds the pack before we can see the header), but we abort after 12 bytes instead of streaming 5 GiB to the target and getting rejected.

So yes — the source still does the work of building the pack. That's unavoidable with git's protocol (there's no "how big would this pack be?" query). But we avoid:
- Downloading the full oversized pack (GiBs of wasted network)
- Pushing it to the target (GiBs of wasted upload)
- The target processing and then rejecting it (GiBs of wasted CPU/IO)

The wasted source-side pack-objects run is typically 10-30 seconds for a large batch — annoying but tolerable, and it only happens when the estimate was wrong (which gets rarer as the checkpoints get finer through subdivision).

# Bootstrap Batching Design

`bootstrap` currently streams one source pack into one target push. That is good for many initial syncs, but it is not enough for very large single-branch repositories where one initial pack is itself too large for comfortable target-side unpacking and indexing.

This note sketches a batching design for large bootstrap jobs.

## Goal

Reduce per-push size and target-side `receive-pack` / `index-pack` pressure for very large initial syncs, while preserving the main benefit of bootstrap:

- no full local object materialization in `git-sync`
- direct source-to-target relay
- clear operator-visible progress and restart points

## Non-Goals

V1 batching should not try to solve every large-migration problem.

Out of scope:

- non-empty target incremental relay batching
- prune/delete behavior
- tag retargeting
- fully optimal pack-size planning
- arbitrary graph partitioning

## Why Per-Ref Batching Is Not Enough

Per-ref batching is easy:

- push `refs/heads/main`
- then `refs/heads/release`
- then tags

That helps when there are many refs and each ref is moderate in size.

It does not help for repositories where a single branch is enormous. Linux `master` is the motivating example: even a single branch bootstrap can be too large for one target-side unpack/index step.

## Preferred Model

Use branch checkpoint batching with temporary refs.

High-level idea:

1. Choose a sequence of ancestor checkpoints for a source branch.
2. Push them oldest to newest into a temporary target ref.
3. Once the final tip is present, create the real target ref.
4. Delete the temporary ref at the end.

input.new_string:
## Checkpoint Selection

Checkpoints are placed using a commit-count estimate, not measured pack sizes.

For a branch tip:

1. Fetch the commit graph (tree:0 filter, one round-trip — commits only, no blobs/trees).
2. Walk first-parent ancestry backward to get the chain length.
3. Estimate total pack size: `chainLen × 8 KiB/commit`.
4. Compute number of batches: `ceil(estimated / --batch-max-pack-bytes)`.
5. Place checkpoints evenly along the first-parent chain.

This is a heuristic — real bytes-per-commit varies widely (2–100+ KiB depending on blob churn). The estimate intentionally errs toward more batches.

### Adaptive size correction

If the estimate is too optimistic (fewer batches than needed), two safeguards catch it:

1. **PACK header pre-check**: after starting a fetch, peek at the first 12 bytes of the pack to read the object count. Multiply by ~750 bytes/object. If the estimate exceeds `--batch-max-pack-bytes`, abort the fetch (12 bytes wasted, not gigabytes), insert a midpoint checkpoint, and retry. This avoids a full transfer for obviously-oversized batches.

2. **Target rejection retry**: if the target's receive-pack rejects a push for exceeding its body-size limit, detect the error, insert a midpoint checkpoint from the stored chain, and retry. This catches cases where the PACK header estimate was close but the real pack was slightly over.

Both safeguards converge in O(log n) splits — each failure halves the commit range.

### Why not probe (the previous design)

The previous implementation did full `FetchPack` round-trips per probe candidate to measure actual pack sizes. For linux/master (75k commits) this required 13+ fetch-and-discard cycles, downloading gigabytes of throwaway data and taking minutes before any real push started. The estimate approach reduces planning to one commit-graph fetch (~20 seconds) plus arithmetic.

## Practical Risks

- the 8 KiB/commit estimate can be significantly off for blob-heavy repos (linux is ~66 KiB/commit); the PACK header pre-check and target-rejection retry handle this adaptively
- object density is not uniform along the commit chain — recent history often has more objects per commit than early history, so evenly-spaced checkpoints produce uneven pack sizes
- target-side unpack/index cost may still be high even after batching, just smaller
- temp refs add cleanup and restart complexity
- the source builds the full pack even if we abort after the PACK header; this wastes source CPU but not network

This is still likely worthwhile for very large initial migrations because it changes a single huge risky operation into several bounded ones.

git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix subdivide retry loop skipping inserted midpoints, update docs

The subdivide-on-failure logic used `continue` inside a `for idx++`
loop, which incremented idx past the newly-inserted midpoint checkpoint
on every retry. The result: infinite retries of the same oversized
range (visible in the linux smoke test as repeated pushes of 6.5M
objects at the same from/to hashes).

Fix: convert to a manual-index loop (`idx := start; for idx < len; ...
idx++` at the bottom). Now `continue` retries at the same idx, where
the checkpoint slice has been rewritten to start with the midpoint.
Both the PACK-header precheck path and the post-push target-rejection
path benefit from the same fix.

- Checkpoint Selection section rewritten to describe the estimate-based
  approach (commit-count heuristic + PACK header precheck + target
  rejection retry) instead of the removed probe-based binary search.

  inaccuracy, non-uniform object density, source-side wasted pack
  builds on PACK header abort).

content.text:
The bug was that `continue` in `for idx := ...; idx < ...; idx++` still runs `idx++`, so every retry skipped the newly-inserted midpoint and pushed the same oversized range forever.

Both sync and replicate go through this same `executeBatched` path when bootstrapping an empty target, so both benefit from all three fixes (estimate planning, PACK header precheck, subdivide retry).

It's working. The adaptive subdivision is converging:

```
6.5M objects → subdivide

862K objects → subdivide again? or push...
```

862K × 750 ≈ 647 MiB — still over your 200 MiB batch limit, so it'll likely subdivide once more to ~430K objects (~322 MiB), then maybe once more to ~215K objects (~161 MiB) which fits. Then it pushes that batch and advances to the next checkpoint.

Each split costs ~20-30s of source pack-objects time (the `Enumerating / Counting / Compressing` you see). The log is honest about what's happening — `old_remaining` → `new_remaining` shows the checkpoint list growing as it finds the right granularity.

Once the first sub-batch lands, the rest will be similarly sized (the chain is being split into roughly equal commit ranges), so subsequent batches should pass the PACK header check on the first try.

The batch-total counter climbing (3 → 4 → 6 → 10) shows the splits accumulating. You're looking at ~10-12 total batches for master instead of the original 3.
hmm, but why do we have it so low as 200mb? the limit is 2gb?

You're right — the `--batch-max-pack-bytes 209715200` (200 MiB) is what you set for the *estimate* planning, but the PACK header precheck is also comparing against `BatchMaxPack`. That means it's subdividing to fit under 200 MiB when the target can actually handle 2 GiB.

Those are two different concerns:

1. **Estimate planning**: how many checkpoints to place upfront (commit-count heuristic)
2. **PACK header precheck**: "will this batch exceed the target's actual limit?"

The precheck should compare against the **target's real limit** (2 GiB), not the planning heuristic. You should be able to set a conservative planning estimate (more batches = smoother progress) while still letting each batch be as large as the target allows.

Quickest fix for your run right now: just raise the flag to match the target:

```bash
--batch-max-pack-bytes 2000000000
```

That gives 1 estimated batch (the estimate thinks 600 MiB fits) and lets the precheck pass anything under 2 GiB.

But the real fix is to separate these two knobs — or better yet, auto-detect the target limit from the error message (which already says `body exceeded size limit 2147483648`). The precheck should use that detected limit, not `BatchMaxPack`.

Want me to add a `--target-max-pack-bytes` flag (or auto-parse from the first rejection), or do you just want to bump the number and finish the linux run for now?
why would I need both attributes now?

You don't. Just set `--batch-max-pack-bytes` to the target's actual limit:

```bash
--batch-max-pack-bytes 2000000000
```

The estimate plans from it (`ceil(75k × 8KiB / 2GiB) = 1 batch`), the PACK header precheck verifies against it, and if anything still slips through, the target rejection retry catches it. One number, three layers of protection.

The 200 MiB was a workaround from before auto-subdivide existed — you had to set it low enough to guarantee every batch fit. Now that's handled automatically.

`--max-pack-bytes` is a separate safety concern (abort if a single source fetch exceeds X total — protection against runaway downloads, not batching). You can drop it entirely for most runs or keep it very high as a circuit breaker.

```bash

  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 2000000000 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-9.log
```
yeah let's move to one `--target-max-pack-bytes` setting then, this is what you meant earlier too, right?
Yes — one flag that means "the target's receive-pack body limit." The system estimates, prechecks, and auto-subdivides from that single number. Let me rename across the codebase.

You are working in /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync. The task is a mechanical rename of the `--batch-max-pack-bytes` flag and its associated internal fields to `--target-max-pack-bytes` / `TargetMaxPackBytes` / `TargetMaxPack`.

This is a pure rename — no behavioral changes.

## What to rename

### CLI flags (cmd/git-sync/main.go)
- `"batch-max-pack-bytes"` → `"target-max-pack-bytes"` (both in runSyncLike and runBootstrap)
- Flag description: change to `"target receive-pack body size limit; batches are planned and auto-subdivided to fit"`
- In the usage string: all occurrences of `--batch-max-pack-bytes` → `--target-max-pack-bytes`

### CLI flags (cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go)
- `"batch-max-pack-bytes"` → `"target-max-pack-bytes"`
- In the usage string: `--batch-max-pack-bytes` → `--target-max-pack-bytes`

### Unstable API (pkg/gitsync/unstable/client.go)
- `AdvancedOptions.BatchMaxPackBytes` → `AdvancedOptions.TargetMaxPackBytes`
- All references to `req.Options.BatchMaxPackBytes` → `req.Options.TargetMaxPackBytes`

### Syncer config (internal/syncer/syncer.go)
- `Config.BatchMaxPackBytes` → `Config.TargetMaxPackBytes`
- `s.cfg.BatchMaxPackBytes` → `s.cfg.TargetMaxPackBytes`

### Bootstrap strategy (internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go)
- `Params.BatchMaxPack` → `Params.TargetMaxPack`
- All `p.BatchMaxPack` → `p.TargetMaxPack`
- Log field name: `"batch_max_pack_bytes"` → `"target_max_pack_bytes"` (there are 2 occurrences)
- `defaultAutoBatchMaxPackBytes` → `defaultTargetMaxPackBytes`
- `autoBatchMaxPackBytes` → `autoTargetMaxPackBytes`

### Test files
- Update all references in test files to match the new field/function names:
  - `internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap_test.go`: `BatchMaxPack` → `TargetMaxPack`, `BatchMaxPackBytes` → `TargetMaxPackBytes`
  - `internal/syncer/integration_test.go`: `BatchMaxPackBytes` → `TargetMaxPackBytes`
  - `cmd/git-sync/main_test.go`: no changes expected (tests use CLI args by string)
  - `cmd/git-sync-bench/*.go`: `BatchMaxPackBytes` → `TargetMaxPackBytes`

### JSON output
- The `syncer.Result` doesn't currently emit BatchMaxPack in JSON, so no JSON field rename needed.

### Docs
- Don't change doc files — I'll handle those separately.

## Verification
After all renames, run:
```
go build ./...
go test ./... -count=1 -timeout 300s
```

Both must pass with zero failures. Do NOT make any behavioral changes — this is purely a rename.

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Rename --batch-max-pack-bytes to --target-max-pack-bytes

With estimate-based planning, PACK header precheck, and auto-subdivide,
there's no longer a reason for the user to think in terms of "batch
sizes." The single meaningful knob is the target's receive-pack body
limit. The system estimates checkpoint placement from it, prechecks
PACK headers against it, and auto-subdivides when batches exceed it.

Rename across CLI flags, internal types, unstable API, docs, and tests:
  --batch-max-pack-bytes  → --target-max-pack-bytes
  BatchMaxPackBytes       → TargetMaxPackBytes
  BatchMaxPack            → TargetMaxPack
  defaultAutoBatchMaxPackBytes → defaultTargetMaxPackBytes
  autoBatchMaxPackBytes   → autoTargetMaxPackBytes

Pure rename, no behavioral changes. All tests pass.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Done. Now retry your linux push with the single flag set to the target's real limit:

```bash
entiredb auth login

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --target-max-pack-bytes 2000000000 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-10.log
```

At 2 GiB target limit: the estimate plans 1 batch (75k × 8KiB = 600 MiB < 2 GiB). The PACK header precheck reads 12 bytes: if object count × 750 > 2 GiB, it auto-subdivides to a midpoint and retries. Converges in a few splits with zero wasted multi-GiB transfers.

    --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
    --batch-max-pack-bytes 1009715200 \
    --max-pack-bytes 21474836480 \
    --measure-memory --stats --json \
    $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-8.log

time=2026-04-14T15:31:04.424+02:00 level=INFO msg="bootstrap batch branch plan" branch=refs/heads/master temp_ref=refs/gitsync/bootstrap/heads/master planned_batches=1 resume_hash=04243787
error: resume bootstrap batch for refs/heads/master: temp ref hash 04243787812cabb57726e50d8288a3dcacf54ad2 does not match any planned checkpoint
exit status 1
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify           44.07s user 3.25s system 100% cpu 47.151 total
tee /tmp/linux-replicate-8.log  0.00s user 0.00s system 0% cpu 47.151 total

The temp ref `refs/gitsync/bootstrap/heads/master` is left over from the earlier 200 MiB run's subdivide — it's at `04243787` (a midpoint the old plan created). The new plan with 1 GiB batch limit only has one checkpoint (the tip), so resume can't match the stale hash.

Clean up the temp refs on the target, then retry:

```bash
# Delete the stale temp refs from the previous run
go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>/dev/null || true

# Or if that doesn't reach them, push deletes directly via git:
git push --delete "$(echo $TGT)" \
  refs/gitsync/bootstrap/heads/master \
  refs/gitsync/bootstrap/heads/nocache-cleanup 2>/dev/null || true
```

Actually, simplest — just probe to see what temp refs exist, then decide:

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --json \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  $SRC $TGT 2>/dev/null | jq '.target.refs | to_entries[] | select(.key | startswith("refs/gitsync"))'
```

This is a known limitation: changing `--target-max-pack-bytes` between runs invalidates the checkpoint plan, and existing temp refs from a different plan cause a resume mismatch. Long-term the planner should handle this by either starting from the stale temp ref's position (it's still a valid point in the chain — the target has all objects up to there) or by deleting it and starting the branch fresh. Want me to add that resilience, or just clean up and retry for now?
can / should we just clear temp refs on a start of a command run then?

Yes — temp refs are an internal implementation detail of bootstrap batching, not user-visible state. If a run starts and finds stale temp refs, it should clean them up and start the branch fresh. The resume logic was designed for crash recovery *within* a single run, not for persistence across runs with different parameters.

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Clear stale bootstrap temp refs instead of failing on resume mismatch

When a temp ref from a previous run doesn't match any planned
checkpoint (e.g., the user changed --target-max-pack-bytes between
runs, or a previous subdivide created midpoints that no longer appear
in the new plan), bootstrap now deletes the stale temp ref and starts
the branch fresh rather than failing with "does not match any planned
checkpoint".

Temp refs are an internal implementation detail of batched bootstrap,
not user-visible state. Treating a mismatch as fatal forced users to
manually delete refs on the target before retrying — unnecessary
friction for a routine parameter change.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Now retry linux — the stale temp refs will be auto-cleaned:

```bash
entiredb auth login

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --target-max-pack-bytes 2000000000 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate-10.log
```

Expected flow:
```
master: stale temp ref at 04243787 → "clearing stale temp ref" → delete → start fresh
master: estimate 1 batch → fetch → PACK header: 11M objects × 750 = 8.2 GiB > 2 GiB
  → subdivide → ~5.5M objects → 4.1 GiB > 2 GiB
  → subdivide → ~2.7M objects → 2.0 GiB → borderline, try push
  → if target accepts: advance, continue
  → if target rejects: subdivide once more → ~1.4M objects → ~1 GiB → push OK
```
